title: Ronnies Party

jokes.text: I'm just joking, I can see they're on.
jokes.setup: Jocelyn says they hope they didn't catch them with their pants down
jokes.punchline: She immediately undercuts the innuendo by noting their pants are visibly on
jokes.explanation: Simple but effective deflation of innuendo. Good economy, but predictable structure.

dialogue.speaker: Jocelyn
dialogue.line: I hope we didn't catch you with your pants down.

dialogue.speaker: Jocelyn
dialogue.line: Your pants are on.

jokes.text: No, we actually listened outside to make sure.
jokes.setup: Following the pants joke, this reveals inappropriate behavior
jokes.punchline: They literally eavesdropped to avoid catching them in compromising position
jokes.explanation: Perfect Roland character beat - inappropriate overshare delivered with complete obliviousness. Strong cringe factor.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: They literally eavesdropped to avoid catching them in compromising position

jokes.text: A lot of pressing of the flesh, and greasing palms, that kind of thing, you know. It wasn't as much fun as it sounds.
jokes.setup: Roland describes political schmoozing using physical metaphors
jokes.punchline: His comment about it not being fun acknowledges the unintended sexual innuendo
jokes.explanation: Roland's oblivious innuendo followed by self-aware deflection. Good character consistency.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: You know, it's like a firm handshake, a good eye contact, maybe a little light touching on the arm. You gotta make 'em feel like they're the only person in the room.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: It's not really fun.

jokes.text: Oh well, who wants an invitation to big soiree when you can be met the very next morning with some table scraps.
jokes.setup: Roland and Jocelyn bring leftovers after not inviting them to party
jokes.punchline: Moira's elaborate sarcastic response elevates the slight into high drama
jokes.explanation: Perfect Moira voice - her theatrical vocabulary and wounded dignity over something trivial. 'Table scraps' is particularly cutting.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Oh, how thoughtful. Leftovers. You know, in some cultures, regifting is considered an art form. In others, it's a cry for help. I'm starting to think yours might be both.

jokes.text: Um, I think it means you have to wear scrubs.
jokes.setup: Alexis asks what 'wear scrubs' really means
jokes.punchline: David's completely literal, obvious response
jokes.explanation: Classic David deadpan - his dry literalism contrasts with Alexis's dramatic overthinking.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: It means to wear scrubs.

jokes.text: Those two are not as simple minded as we thought. He is.
jokes.setup: Moira starts to give Roland and Jocelyn credit
jokes.punchline: Johnny immediately clarifies that Roland is exactly as simple-minded as they thought
jokes.explanation: Excellent economy and structure. Perfect setup/punchline timing with Johnny's practical correction to Moira's assessment.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: You know, I've been thinking... Roland and Jocelyn have been quite helpful to us.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Moira, no. Let's not get carried away. Roland is exactly as simple-minded as we thought.

jokes.text: Whatever happened to empowering women's sexuality?
jokes.setup: Discussion about nursing uniforms
jokes.punchline: Alexis turns medical scrubs into a feminist fashion issue
jokes.explanation: Perfectly captures Alexis's tendency to make everything about her worldview. The disconnect between medical practicality and sexual empowerment is absurd but character-appropriate.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: I'm not wearing those scrubs. They're so unflattering and they come in, like, hospital blue? I need them in a color that doesn't make me look like I'm actively dying.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Alexis, they're medical uniforms, not a capsule wardrobe.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Exactly! Which is why this is a feminist issue. We're forcing women to wear these shapeless, soul-crushing garments that strip away their individuality and femininity. It's basically a form of oppression.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: You're a receptionist.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: A receptionist with rights, David.

jokes.text: Obviously you've never been out for Halloween, David.
jokes.setup: David says sex appeal isn't the point of nursing uniforms
jokes.punchline: Alexis references sexy nurse costumes as counter-evidence
jokes.explanation: Good character moment showing Alexis's pop culture perspective overriding practical considerations.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Sex appeal isn't the point of nursing uniforms.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Um, have you ever seen a sexy nurse costume?

jokes.text: Putting your name on a line of edible nail polish isn't what I would call having a job.
jokes.setup: Alexis claims this isn't her first job
jokes.punchline: David reveals her previous 'job' was edible nail polish
jokes.explanation: Excellent character detail that's both absurd and believable for Alexis's privileged background. 'Edible nail polish' is wonderfully specific and ridiculous.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: This isn't my first job, David.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Oh really? And what was your previous job?

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: I was a taste tester for edible nail polish.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: That's not a job, Alexis. That's just you eating nail polish.

jokes.text: Even the one that poisoned all those people?
jokes.setup: Alexis defends her hands-on involvement in creating flavors
jokes.punchline: David reveals the product line had a poisoning incident
jokes.explanation: Perfect escalation that takes the absurd premise to its logical dark conclusion. The casual mention of poisoning people is brilliantly dark.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: I was very hands-on with creating the flavors. I tested every single one.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Yes, well, that's actually something we need to talk about. The product line had a poisoning incident.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: What? From my flavors?

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Well, let's just say your hands-on approach may have been a bit too hands-on.

jokes.text: David, the factory in Guangzhou assured us that it was lead-free.
jokes.setup: David mentions people getting poisoned
jokes.punchline: Alexis's defense makes it worse by mentioning lead and foreign manufacturing
jokes.explanation: Alexis's attempt at defense only makes the situation sound worse. The specific detail about lead-free assurance from Guangzhou adds authenticity.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: People are getting poisoned.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Well, it's not like we're using lead paint or anything. It's all imported from overseas.

jokes.text: You look like a stick of gum.
jokes.setup: Alexis is presumably wearing pink scrubs
jokes.punchline: David's blunt visual comparison
jokes.explanation: Simple visual joke with good economy. David's fashion criticism is character-appropriate.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: You look like a flamingo that got into a car accident.

jokes.text: She's an IT manager, Gerald. She makes more money than both of us combined.
jokes.setup: Conversation about someone taking money
jokes.punchline: Stevie points out the absurdity of worrying about someone who's wealthy
jokes.explanation: Stevie's practical voice cutting through Gerald's worry. Good character consistency.

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: I mean, why are you worried about him? He's rich.

jokes.text: four more taxidermy peacocks coming this afternoon
jokes.setup: Discussion about expenses
jokes.punchline: The specific absurdity of multiple taxidermy peacocks as a business expense
jokes.explanation: Wonderfully specific absurd detail. Four taxidermy peacocks is exactly the kind of ridiculous expense that fits the show's world.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: These taxidermy peacocks are a legitimate business expense.

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: How are multiple dead birds a business expense?

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: They're for the store's ambiance. It's called branding.

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: You have five of them.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Yes, well, one peacock says 'rustic.' Five peacocks say 'I have a problem.'

jokes.text: I wasn't sure what the etiquette was for this kind of thing, so I walked around the block a little bit.
jokes.setup: Alexis arrives at her new job
jokes.punchline: She treated showing up for work like a social call requiring careful timing
jokes.explanation: Perfect Alexis moment - overthinking work etiquette through the lens of social events. Shows her complete inexperience with employment.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: I'm not arriving too early, that's desperate. And I'm not arriving too late, that's rude. I'm arriving at the exact moment that says, 'I have a life, but I'm also very responsible.'

jokes.text: Oh. I thought this was kind of an either/or thing.
jokes.setup: Ted asks where the rest of her scrubs are
jokes.punchline: Alexis thought scrub tops and bottoms were optional alternatives
jokes.explanation: Good character moment showing Alexis's fashion-forward interpretation of medical uniforms.

dialogue.speaker: Ted
dialogue.line: Where are the rest of your scrubs?

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: What do you mean? I'm wearing scrubs.

dialogue.speaker: Ted
dialogue.line: You're only wearing the top.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Well, I thought the top and bottom were optional alternatives. Like, you could wear one or the other.

jokes.text: Those bare legs are dangerous. Ted! No, I'm serious. The animals carry bugs or ticks that can burrow into your bare skin.
jokes.setup: Ted mentions dangerous bare legs
jokes.punchline: Alexis's flirtatious reaction followed by Ted's medical reality check
jokes.explanation: Good misdirection where Alexis misinterprets Ted's practical concern as flirtation before the gross reality sets in.

dialogue.speaker: Ted
dialogue.line: You really shouldn't expose your legs like that. It's dangerous.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Oh Ted, are you saying my legs are dangerous?

dialogue.speaker: Ted
dialogue.line: No, I'm saying that you're exposing yourself to the sun without protection, and you're at risk for skin cancer, premature aging, and sun poisoning.

jokes.text: You won't even miss your last secretary once I get finished with these puppies.
jokes.setup: Alexis is enthusiastic about filing
jokes.punchline: Her overconfident promise about surpassing the previous secretary
jokes.explanation: Classic dramatic irony - Alexis's confidence about basic filing foreshadows her inevitable struggles.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: I'm going to be such a good secretary. I'm honestly going to be better than the last one. I'm going to file things. I'm going to file them so hard.

jokes.text: New khakis I see.
jokes.setup: Moira greeting Ronnie
jokes.punchline: Her focus on fashion details in casual conversation
jokes.explanation: Minor character beat showing Moira's fashion awareness even in small talk.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Ronnie, darling. That jacket is absolutely stunning. Is that a custom Armani?

dialogue.speaker: Ronnie
dialogue.line: It's from the Walmart.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Well, it's very... functional. The pleating alone suggests someone understands the importance of structure. Though I would have perhaps suggested a more refined fabric. Cashmere? Silk-blend? Anything really that whispers rather than shouts.

jokes.text: I was. - Good. Lots of dairy.
jokes.setup: Moira asks about Jocelyn's party
jokes.punchline: Ronnie's minimalist, unappetizing summary
jokes.explanation: Perfect Ronnie economy - reducing a social event to its most basic, unappealing element.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: So what was Jocelyn's party like?

dialogue.speaker: Ronnie
dialogue.line: It was fine. She had some cheese. It wasn't good cheese.

jokes.text: If anyone knows how to put a lactose intolerant in the hospital, it's our Jocelyn.
jokes.setup: Discussion of dairy-heavy party
jokes.punchline: Moira's elaborate way of criticizing Jocelyn's hosting
jokes.explanation: Classic Moira - turning dietary intolerance into dramatic medical emergency. Her elaborate vocabulary for simple criticism is perfect character work.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Well, I think what you've done here is very... dairy-forward. Which is interesting, because I find that when one hosts, one typically considers the lactose-intolerant contingent. But what do I know? I'm just someone who's thrown dinner parties for heads of state.

jokes.text: Political weight. I now realize 'clout' was the word I was looking for.
jokes.setup: Moira asks Ronnie to put her weight behind her
jokes.punchline: Her self-correction acknowledging the unintended insult
jokes.explanation: Good character moment showing Moira's verbal pretensions getting her into trouble, followed by rare self-awareness.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Ronnie, could you put your weight behind me?

dialogue.speaker: Ronnie
dialogue.line: [reaction]

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: No, I meant— your physical strength. Not your... mass.

jokes.text: It's a date. No. No, it's not. I wouldn't call it that.
jokes.setup: Moira says 'it's a date' about the political meeting
jokes.punchline: Ronnie's immediate rejection and Moira's flustered backpedaling
jokes.explanation: Good cringe moment with both characters uncomfortable. Shows the awkwardness of Moira's social missteps.

dialogue.speaker: Ronnie
dialogue.line: It's not a date.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: No, of course not. I just meant... it's a thing. A meeting thing. That we're both attending.

jokes.text: Like I once dated this Sultan's nephew who was forbidden to talk to me or even to look at me and we made it work for like half a regime change, so...
jokes.setup: Alexis giving relationship advice about overcoming obstacles
jokes.punchline: Her example involves international politics and royal restrictions
jokes.explanation: Absolutely perfect Alexis moment. The specificity of 'half a regime change' as a relationship timeline is brilliant. Shows her globetrotting past while treating geopolitics casually.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: You know, it's like when you really love someone, you just have to find a way to make it work, no matter what obstacles are in your way. Like, my friend's cousin dated this guy who was basically royalty in another country, and there were all these international laws and political issues keeping them apart, but they just... they figured it out. Well, actually, no, they didn't. He had to marry someone else for like, a trade agreement or something. But the point is, they tried!

jokes.text: I have been swamped. Doris's cat is like totally high maintenance.
jokes.setup: Ted asks about filing progress
jokes.punchline: Alexis confuses personal counseling with professional duties
jokes.explanation: Good character work showing Alexis's complete misunderstanding of professional priorities.

dialogue.speaker: Ted
dialogue.line: How's the filing coming along?

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Oh my god, so good. I've been really getting into it. I organized all the files by color, and then by texture, and then I realized that I was just going through a really difficult time emotionally, so I've been using work as a way to avoid dealing with my feelings.

dialogue.speaker: Ted
dialogue.line: ...That's great, Alexis.

jokes.text: Yeah, well, Doris has been worried about her cat for two years. She comes in every week.
jokes.setup: Alexis treating Doris's cat concerns as urgent
jokes.punchline: Ted reveals this is a chronic, ongoing situation
jokes.explanation: Good reality check showing Alexis's lack of context about ongoing client relationships.

dialogue.speaker: Ted
dialogue.line: Alexis, I appreciate your concern, but Doris has been bringing Mr. Whiskers in here every week for the past six months convinced he has a different terminal illness.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Well, maybe he does!

dialogue.speaker: Ted
dialogue.line: Last week it was feline leukemia. The week before, a brain tumor. Two weeks ago, she was certain he'd been poisoned.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Oh my God.

dialogue.speaker: Ted
dialogue.line: He's fine, Alexis. He's a very healthy cat. But his owner is... a lot.

jokes.text: I need someone to take care of my stepdaughter. I need you to take care of my stepdaughter.
jokes.setup: David offers vague help
jokes.punchline: Stevie's increasingly direct demands, ignoring his hints
jokes.explanation: Excellent escalation showing Stevie bulldozing through David's polite deflection. The repetition builds the comedy.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: I'm here if you need anything.

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: Okay, I need you to pick up my prescription.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Oh, I meant more like... emotionally.

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: Great. Pick up my prescription emotionally then.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: I was trying to give you space to—

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: David, pick up my prescription.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Yes, okay.

jokes.text: I don't think that's a very good idea. I'm gonna drop her off at four.
jokes.setup: David tries to decline babysitting
jokes.punchline: Stevie completely ignores his objection and proceeds with the plan
jokes.explanation: Perfect power dynamic - Stevie's desperation overriding David's very reasonable concerns.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: I can't babysit. I have plans.

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: Great. You're babysitting tomorrow at seven.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Did you hear what I just said?

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: I did. Babysitting. Tomorrow. Seven.

jokes.text: I brought you some trail mix, but I spilled it in my purse.
jokes.setup: Doris bringing a thoughtful gift
jokes.punchline: The gift is now contaminated and useless
jokes.explanation: Good character moment showing well-meaning gesture gone wrong. Relatable minor disaster.

jokes.text: Ew! I'm just trying to figure out what happened, all right?
jokes.setup: Doris asks about her dead cat
jokes.punchline: Ted's frustrated reaction to having to deal with death while maintaining professional demeanor
jokes.explanation: Ted's professional stress showing through his usual calm demeanor.

dialogue.speaker: Doris
dialogue.line: My cat died. What do I do with the body?

dialogue.speaker: Ted
dialogue.line: I... well, there are pet cremation services, or you could have a burial in your yard, or—

dialogue.speaker: Ted
dialogue.line: Why does everyone keep dying?

jokes.text: Sounds like something you'd find at a serial killer's house.
jokes.setup: Stevie describes puzzle pieces in a Rummoli box
jokes.punchline: David's dark interpretation of the innocent description
jokes.explanation: Perfect David neurosis - turning innocent game supplies into horror movie props. Good dark comedy.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: So what you're saying is... we're trapped in a box with broken dreams and false hope?

jokes.text: You're joking. But you hate kids. Oh my God. Can I watch? Or video tape? Can I bring friends?
jokes.setup: David reveals he's babysitting
jokes.punchline: Stevie's immediate recognition that this will be a disaster worth documenting
jokes.explanation: Excellent escalation showing Stevie recognizing the entertainment value of David's certain failure. Her enthusiasm is infectious.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: I'm babysitting tonight.

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: Oh my God, whose child did you agree to this?

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: Wait, don't answer that. I need to get my phone ready to document this.

jokes.text: If you happen to find any Xanax lying around anywhere, can you just... let me know about that?
jokes.setup: David asking Stevie to look for something at the motel
jokes.punchline: His specific request for anxiety medication
jokes.explanation: Good character moment showing David's anxiety about babysitting. The casual request for prescription drugs is appropriately concerning and funny.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Stevie, when you're at the motel, can you check the lost and found for my anxiety medication?

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: David, that's not how anxiety medication works.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Well, I'm anxious that I lost it, so technically it is.

jokes.text: Yeah, he's dead. But! But the good news is, you're not.
jokes.setup: Doris asking about her cat
jokes.punchline: Alexis's attempt to find a positive spin on pet death
jokes.explanation: Brilliantly inappropriate attempt at positive thinking. Alexis's tone-deaf optimism in the face of grief is perfectly in character and darkly funny.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Well, the good news is that Mr. Whiskers is no longer suffering from feline diabetes.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: And he's finally at a weight where he could fit through a standard doorway.

jokes.text: But you're free, Doris, and, in a way, so is Mittens.
jokes.setup: Trying to comfort Doris about her dead cat
jokes.punchline: Alexis treating pet death as spiritual liberation
jokes.explanation: Perfect Alexis philosophy - turning pet death into self-help speak. Her ability to find meaning in everything is both touching and ridiculous.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Okay, so Fluffy is like, totally in a better place now. Like, she's been released from the physical constraints of her body, and she's like, spiritually transcended or whatever. She's probably like, really happy right now, you know? Like, she's experiencing ultimate freedom.

dialogue.speaker: Doris
dialogue.line: She was hit by a car.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Exactly! So like, one minute she's stuck in this earthly realm, and the next minute she's just like, whoooosh, gone. It's actually really beautiful when you think about it.

jokes.text: I think you just approach these women like you did Paula, our landscaper. Very strong, very forceful.
jokes.setup: Moira giving Johnny advice for the meeting
jokes.punchline: Her inappropriate suggestion to be 'forceful' with women, plus random landscaper reference
jokes.explanation: Sets up the inappropriate behavior advice that will backfire. The landscaper detail adds to Moira's scattered thinking.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: You need to be forceful with women, Johnny. They respect a man who takes charge. Now, I once knew a landscaper named—actually, never mind, that's not relevant.

jokes.text: Um, yeah, you can always tell because their upper lip sort of... I mean, like, puffs a bit.
jokes.setup: Discussion about plastic surgery
jokes.punchline: David's specific expertise about cosmetic procedures
jokes.explanation: Good character moment showing David's fashion/beauty knowledge extending to surgical tells.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: I mean, I've done extensive research on cosmetic procedures. I've practically memorized every before and after photo on the internet. I could tell you the difference between a chemical peel and a microdermabrasion in my sleep. Not that I would ever need either one, obviously.

jokes.text: Something happened. Nothing. Just d-don't look on the bed.
jokes.setup: Child asking if David is okay
jokes.punchline: His panicked warning about something on the bed
jokes.explanation: Excellent cringe moment suggesting David has already had a babysitting disaster. The audience's imagination fills in the horror.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: There's a bug on the bed! There's a bug on the bed! Oh my God, there's a bug on the bed!

jokes.text: Needless to say, that was the last time I played charades with Fran Leibovitz.
jokes.setup: Johnny telling an anecdote to the women
jokes.punchline: Casual mention of playing charades with famous writer
jokes.explanation: Good character moment showing Johnny's past connections with cultural figures, casually dropped into conversation.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: I was at a dinner party last week, and we ended up playing charades. David Sedaris was there, and let me tell you, that man cannot act. He was trying to do 'Breakfast at Tiffany's' and nobody could guess it. I finally said, 'David, you're a professional writer—surely you can convey a simple concept through physical movement.' He just looked at me and said, 'I'm better with words.'

jokes.text: No, I was just talking someone over there who has a husband. A male husband.
jokes.setup: Johnny trying to correct Moira's assumption about lesbian gathering
jokes.punchline: Specification of 'male husband' as if that's unusual clarification needed
jokes.explanation: Johnny's awkward attempt to be inclusive while correcting Moira's assumptions

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: No, it's not a lesbian gathering. It's just a group of women who are interested in the same things.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Oh, so it's a book club?

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: No, it's not a book club. It's just... a gathering.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Well, then what do they have in common?

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: They all have male husbands.

jokes.text: I knew I should've left him home with the sitter.
jokes.setup: Johnny having social difficulties at the political event
jokes.punchline: Moira treating her adult husband like a misbehaving child
jokes.explanation: Perfect Moira moment - her dramatic response to Johnny's social awkwardness. The reversal of traditional gender roles is well-executed.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Johnny, what did I tell you about talking to the mayor's wife?

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: I was just making conversation.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: You asked her about her bunions, John. Her bunions.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: She brought them up!

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: And now you're going to stand in the corner and think about what you've done.

jokes.text: Moira, I have a feeling not every woman here is a lesbian.
jokes.setup: Johnny's confusion about the demographic
jokes.punchline: Revealing his assumption about the 'key demographic'
jokes.explanation: Good reveal of Johnny's misunderstanding. Shows his assumption based on Moira's earlier hints.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: What is the key demographic?

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Women, 18 to 49.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Oh. I thought you meant a key demographic. Like someone who has a key.

jokes.text: Entrepreneurs. Everyone here is a member of the Women's Business Association.
jokes.setup: The big reveal about the demographic
jokes.punchline: The anticlimactic truth about the group
jokes.explanation: Good payoff to the misdirection setup. The mundane reality is funnier than the assumed situation.

dialogue.speaker: Ronnie
dialogue.line: You want to know who your core demographic is? It's people who have nothing better to do than watch you fail.

jokes.text: how many two-week vacations are available per year?
jokes.setup: Alexis asking about vacation days
jokes.punchline: Her assumption that she'd get multiple two-week vacations
jokes.explanation: Perfect Alexis entitlement - treating entry-level work like a luxury lifestyle. Shows her complete disconnect from normal employment.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: So how many vacation days do I get?

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Two weeks a year.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Right, and then how many more after that?

jokes.text: Nothing is more natural... aside from maybe the... thread count in these sheets
jokes.setup: David giving advice about puberty
jokes.punchline: His inappropriate focus on luxury bedding during the conversation
jokes.explanation: Brilliant David moment - even during an awkward puberty talk, he can't help but critique home furnishings. Perfect character priorities.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Look, puberty is a natural part of growing up. Your body is going to change, you're going to have new feelings, and it's all perfectly normal.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: The important thing is to take care of yourself. Invest in a good skincare routine, obviously. And Egyptian cotton sheets—at least 1000 thread count. Trust me, when you're going through all these hormonal changes, you want to be sleeping on something that feels like a cloud.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Seriously, I cannot stress this enough. Polyester blends are going to make everything worse. You need to find a luxury linen supplier immediately.

jokes.text: You did not choose this life! No, each one of you was born to be an entrepreneur.
jokes.setup: Moira trying to appeal to business women
jokes.punchline: Her dramatic declaration treating business ownership like sexual orientation
jokes.explanation: Excellent Moira moment - her confused application of civil rights rhetoric to business ownership. Shows her complete misunderstanding while maintaining her theatrical delivery.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: I'm a business owner. I came out as a business owner.

jokes.text: I had a six-and-a-half season career as an actress in television, which I put on hold to devote all my time and energy to raise two million dollars for charity. And two children.
jokes.setup: Moira describing her work experience
jokes.punchline: Her prioritization of money over children in the list
jokes.explanation: Perfect Moira priorities - mentioning charity money before her own children. The 'six-and-a-half season' specificity is great character detail.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: I've had many jobs over the years. I was a spokesperson for a medical device company, a hostess, a real estate agent, and I once prioritized money over children.

jokes.text: you, Tara, you're very concerned about your inverted nipples. Which I assumed was public domain; there were a lot of you around when she told me.
jokes.setup: Moira trying to show personal connection with constituents
jokes.punchline: Her public mention of private medical information
jokes.explanation: Brilliant cringe comedy - Moira's complete lack of social boundaries. Her defense that it's 'public domain' makes it worse.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: I was at the grocery store the other day, and I ran into one of my constituents. Such a lovely woman. We chatted for a bit, and I mentioned my recent bout with hemorrhoids.

jokes.text: Black? No. I mean that everyone was leaning... Younger?
jokes.setup: Johnny trying to explain his assumption about the women's group
jokes.punchline: His fumbling attempts to find a non-offensive way to describe his misconception
jokes.explanation: Decent cringe moment but fairly standard awkward backpedaling

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: I just assumed, you know, that it was a group of... well, I thought it was going to be a more... how do I put this delicately... a more estrogen-based organization.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Not that there's anything wrong with that! I just... I pictured it as being more of a, you know, a feminine gathering.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: What I'm trying to say is, I made an assumption based on the name, and clearly I was mistaken, which is embarrassing.

jokes.text: I had my first job at the age of ten, sweeping up hair in a beauty salon.
jokes.setup: Ronnie sharing her work history
jokes.punchline: The contrast between her humble beginnings and the Rose family's privilege
jokes.explanation: Good character detail showing the class divide. Ronnie's genuine work history contrasts with the Roses' privileged background.

dialogue.speaker: Ronnie
dialogue.line: I started out working in construction, doing manual labor. Then I moved into heavy equipment operation. Now I'm running this real estate business.

dialogue.speaker: Ronnie
dialogue.line: And you people came here with nothing but designer luggage and a country club membership that doesn't work anymore.

jokes.text: I am very comfortable seeing women with other women. - Uh-huh. - Yeah, that didn't come out right.
jokes.setup: Johnny trying to prove he's not prejudiced
jokes.punchline: His statement coming out wrong in a different way
jokes.explanation: Standard awkward moment but not particularly original or memorable

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: I'm not prejudiced. I have a very diverse group of friends.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: I have a Black friend, an Asian friend, a gay friend...

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Well, actually, my gay friend is also Black, so that's two birds with one stone.

jokes.text: that I am very comfortable seeing women with other women.
jokes.setup: Johnny trying to show he's progressive
jokes.punchline: His inappropriate oversharing about lesbian relationships
jokes.explanation: More cringe as Johnny digs himself deeper trying to prove his progressive credentials inappropriately.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: You know, I'm very progressive. I have several lesbian friends. Well, I don't know them personally, but I've seen documentaries.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: And I understand that in lesbian relationships, one of them is typically the man. Which I find fascinating.

jokes.text: Oh, the dip was incredible, really, for men and women.
jokes.setup: Johnny trying to end on a positive note
jokes.punchline: His unnecessary gendering of food appreciation
jokes.explanation: Final cringe moment as Johnny tries to recover but continues unnecessarily categorizing things by gender.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Look, I know I've been hard on this town, but I want you to know that I appreciate what you've all done for us. And I'm going to try to be more positive going forward. So, moving ahead, I'm going to make a real effort to embrace the good things in life. You know, like a good meal. A delicious meal. Because there's nothing quite like sitting down to a really exquisite plate of food and just... savoring it. Mmm. Yes. And I think that's something that a man can really appreciate.

tmdb_overview: Johnny and Moira meet with a key group of people, Alexis struggles with her new job, David offer's emotional support to his boss' daughter.
insight: Ronnie's abrasive charm carries a solid mid-season pivot with 53 jokes at steady 2.45-per-minute pace.
